does: This paper identifies and empirically demonstrates a new category of jailbreak attack where adversaries query multiple large language models simultaneously rather than targeting a single model. The authors develop a novel jailbreak method specifically optimized for this wide-net-casting scenario, showing that by strategically distributing adversarial queries across a group of models, attackers can achieve near-perfect success rates in eliciting harmful outputs even from models without additional safeguards. The work provides systematic analysis of the safety vulnerabilities exposed when models are evaluated collectively rather than in isolation, with experimental validation across multiple state-of-the-art large language models.
connects: This work extends the growing literature on adversarial attacks and jailbreaking of large language models, building on prior research into prompt injection, adversarial prompts, and safety bypassing techniques. While previous jailbreak research has primarily focused on single-model scenarios and individual adversarial techniques, this paper responds to the practical reality that real-world deployments involve multiple accessible models and that safety evaluations typically assess models in isolation. The wide-net-casting approach parallels ensemble attack methods studied in computer vision and adversarial ML more broadly, adapting these multi-model attack strategies to the language domain where ensemble diversity and safety properties create new vulnerabilities.
enables: This work points toward critical directions in multilateral AI safety evaluation and defense. It highlights the need for safety benchmarks that assess models not just individually but under scenarios where adversaries have access to multiple systems simultaneously, potentially shifting evaluation paradigms. Future work could develop distributed defense mechanisms that coordinate safety responses across multiple models, design detection systems for ensemble-based attacks, and create training procedures that increase robustness against wide-net-casting scenarios. The findings also motivate research into why model diversity, intended as a safety feature, can paradoxically create vulnerabilities when models are queried together, potentially informing better ensemble design for safety-critical applications.
